summary: Spanish Baroque painter from Seville. Known for genre scenes of street children (The Young Beggar, Boys Eating Grapes and Melon, Two Women at a Window) and numerous depictions of the Immaculate Conception. Cultivated a diffuse 'vaporous style' (estilo vaporoso). Died after falling from scaffolding.

works.name: Immaculate Conception paintings
works.description: Nearly twenty versions of the Immaculate Conception, featuring Mary in a blue cloak surrounded by cherubs. The Immaculate Conception of Los Venerables was possibly the most expensive painting in the world when the Louvre bought it from Marshal Soult's estate. 'The Colossal' version unusually features a full moon at the bottom with cherubs struggling to hold up a cloud. References to Marian litanies are absent from his versions. Exemplifies his estilo vaporoso. Wikipedia

works.name: Genre Scenes (Street Children)
works.description: Famous for paintings of beggars and street urchins of Seville. The Young Beggar, Boys Eating Grapes and Melon (or Children Eating Grapes and a Melon), The Little Fruit Seller.

clues.clue: The Young Beggar and Boys Eating Grapes and Melon: genre scenes of street children / beggars of Seville
examples: painted The Young Beggar and Children Eating Grapes and a Melon
Two poor children sumptuously devour grapes and melons in this artist's best known genre-scene

clues.clue: The Little Fruit Seller: another genre scene of contemporary life
examples: This artist's works depicting contemporary life include The Young Beggar and The Little Fruit Seller

works.name: Two Women at a Window (A Girl and Her Duena)
works.description: A leering/flirtatious woman lifts her veil while a somber bespectacled woman (duena) holds the head of a boy. Also described as a woman covering her laugh with her mantilla shawl. Depicts Galician-born prostitutes in Madrid.

clues.clue: Woman resting chin on hand next to woman covering mouth with veil/mantilla; one invitingly lifts her veil while bespectacled woman looks on; boy's butt exposed to viewer
examples: showed a woman resting her chin on her hand next to a woman covering her mouth with her veil in his painting Two Women at a Window
a leering woman invitingly lifts her veil next to a stone-cold somber bespectacled woman holding the head of a boy whose butt is exposed to the viewer
A flirtatious woman at a window covers her laugh with her mantilla shawl in this artist's painting A Girl and Her Duena, one of his many depictions of Galician-born prostitutes in Madrid

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Spanish Baroque painter from Seville. Contemporary of Velazquez. Cultivated 'cool,' 'warm,' and 'vaporous' (estilo vaporoso) styles depending on degree of tenebrism. Early career influenced by Zurbaran (the 'Spanish Caravaggio'). Died after falling from scaffolding at a church in Cadiz while painting Mystic Marriage of St. Catherine. Painted St. Isidore of Seville (patron saint of the Internet) for Seville Cathedral.

comprehensive_summary:
Bartolome Esteban Murillo (1617-1682) was a Spanish Baroque painter from Seville, a contemporary of Velazquez. His paintings are categorized into three styles based on degree of tenebrism: 'cool,' 'warm,' and 'vaporous' (estilo vaporoso), with the vaporous style representing his mature work with soft, diffuse tones.

He is best known for his nearly twenty versions of the Immaculate Conception, which feature Mary in a blue cloak surrounded by cherubs with putti blending into the background like haze. The Immaculate Conception of Los Venerables was possibly the most expensive painting in the world when the Louvre purchased it from Marshal Soult's estate. 'The Colossal' version unusually features a full moon at the bottom with cherubs struggling to hold up a cloud. Unlike other painters' versions, his omit references to the Marian litanies.

His genre scenes of the street children and beggars of Seville are equally famous. The Young Beggar and Boys Eating Grapes and Melon depict poor children in vivid detail. Two Women at a Window (A Girl and Her Duena) shows a flirtatious woman lifting her veil next to a somber bespectacled duena, depicting Galician-born prostitutes in Madrid.

Murillo's early career was influenced by Zurbaran (the 'Spanish Caravaggio'), and like Velazquez, he lightened his use of tenebrism later in his career. He created the most widely circulated portrait of St. Isidore of Seville (patron saint of the Internet) for Seville Cathedral. Other works include Vision of St. Anthony, Saints Justa and Rufina, Young Man with a Basket of Fruit, and the Heavenly and Earthly Trinities. A portrait attributed to him features concentric circles representing the heliocentric model, linked to the Galileo legend of 'Eppur si muove.' He died after falling from scaffolding while painting the Mystic Marriage of St. Catherine for a Capuchin church in Cadiz.
